seoTitle: Top 5 Medicare Supplement Plans in 2026 Compared
seoDescription: Wondering what are the top 5 Medicare Supplement plans? Compare Plan G, N, F, A, and High Deductible G to find the best fit for your budget.

sections.text: Making Sense of Medicare Supplement Plans

sections.content: Ten letters. Dozens of insurance companies. Nearly identical coverage descriptions that seem to blur together the moment you try to compare them side by side. It is no surprise that so many people put off choosing a Medigap plan simply because the process feels harder than it should be.
This guide breaks down the five most commonly chosen Medicare Supplement plans in plain language, without the jargon that usually clouds these comparisons.
The letter on a plan matters less than what it means for your specific situation. Your doctors, your prescriptions, and your monthly budget should guide the decision, not the alphabet.

sections.text: Why Medicare Supplement Terms Feel Confusing at First

sections.content: Every Medigap plan with the same letter offers the same core benefits, no matter which insurance company sells it or which state you live in. That standardization is meant to simplify comparison shopping, but it can feel counterintuitive at first since the letters themselves carry no obvious meaning.

It also helps to separate Medigap from Medicare Advantage, since the two are frequently confused. Medigap works alongside Original Medicare to cover gaps like coinsurance and deductibles, while Medicare Advantage replaces Original Medicare with a private plan that often includes its own network and rules.

sections.calloutText: Because Medigap plans are standardized by letter, a Plan G policy from one insurer covers the same core benefits as a Plan G policy from another. Price and customer service are usually the biggest differences between carriers.

sections.content: Once you understand this structure, comparing plans becomes far more manageable. You are really just comparing five familiar coverage patterns rather than an endless list of confusing options.

sections.text: The Top 5 Medicare Supplement Plans Explained

sections.content: Plan G, Plan N, Plan F, Plan A, and High Deductible Plan G make up the five plans most people choose today. Each one strikes a different balance between monthly premium and out-of-pocket cost.
Plan F is closed to anyone who became eligible for Medicare on or after January 1, 2020, which is an important detail if you are newly eligible and comparing older comparison charts online.

sections.text: Plan G: Comprehensive Coverage With One Deductible

sections.content: Plan G covers nearly all the gaps left by Original Medicare, including Part A coinsurance, hospital costs, and Part B excess charges. The only cost you are responsible for is the annual Part B deductible, which is $283 in 2026.
This predictability is why so many people gravitate toward Plan G. Once you meet that single deductible, your covered costs for the rest of the year become easy to anticipate.

sections.text: Plan N: Lower Premiums With Small Copays

sections.content: Plan N typically carries a lower monthly premium than Plan G in exchange for small copays, usually up to $20 for office visits and up to $50 for emergency room visits that do not result in admission. It also does not cover Part B excess charges.
This plan tends to fit people who do not visit the doctor frequently and are comfortable trading a bit of predictability for a lower monthly bill.

sections.text: Plan F: Full Coverage for Those Still Eligible

sections.content: Plan F is only available to people who became eligible for Medicare before January 1, 2020. For those who qualify, it remains the most complete coverage option available, paying the Part B deductible in addition to everything Plan G covers.
If you already have Plan F, you can generally keep it. If you are newly eligible, this option will not appear on your list of choices.

Plan A includes the core Medigap benefits, such as Part A coinsurance and hospital costs, and Part B coinsurance. It leaves out extras like the Part B deductible, excess charges, and foreign travel emergency coverage.
This plan often appeals to people looking for basic protection at the lowest possible premium, particularly those in good health who want to limit monthly expenses.

sections.text: High Deductible Plan G: Lower Premium, Higher Deductible Trade-Off

sections.content: High Deductible Plan G works exactly like standard Plan G once you meet the annual deductible, which is $2,950 in 2026. Until that deductible is met, you pay Medicare-covered costs out of pocket.

This option fits people comfortable paying more upfront if something happens, in exchange for a noticeably lower monthly premium. It can work well for those in good health with some savings set aside for medical costs.

I always tell people to picture a full year of care, not just next month's bill, before choosing between Plan G and Plan N. A slightly higher premium on Plan G often evens out once you factor in the copays you would otherwise pay with Plan N or the deductible exposure with the high deductible version.

sections.text: Comparing Costs: Premiums, Deductibles, and Out-of-Pocket Expenses

sections.content: Premiums for the same lettered plan can vary significantly depending on where you live, your age, and which insurance company you choose. Two people with Plan G in different states could pay very different monthly amounts for identical coverage.

Deductibles and copays are where the five plans truly separate from one another. Plan G and Plan F cap your Part B exposure to essentially nothing, Plan N introduces small office and ER copays, Plan A leaves the Part B deductible fully exposed, and High Deductible Plan G shifts a much larger amount of cost to you before coverage kicks in.

sections.title: 2026 Medigap Plan Comparison at a Glance
rows: Up to $20 office, up to $50 ER
Those eligible before 2020 wanting full coverage
Lower premium, comfortable with upfront risk
sections.footnote: Premiums vary by carrier, state, and age, so use this table for coverage structure rather than exact dollar comparisons.

sections.calloutText: Compare total yearly cost, not just the monthly premium. A lower premium plan can end up costing more once copays and deductibles are added up over twelve months of care.

sections.text: Will Your Doctors and Prescriptions Still Be Covered

sections.content: Medigap plans work alongside Original Medicare rather than replacing it, which means any doctor or hospital that accepts Medicare will accept your Medigap plan. There is no network to worry about, so switching plans does not put your relationship with a trusted specialist at risk.

Prescription drug coverage is handled separately through a standalone Part D plan, since none of the five Medigap plans above include drug benefits. The average Part D premium in 2026 is $34.50, though your specific plan and drug list will affect your actual cost.
If keeping your current specialist is a top priority, Medigap generally offers more peace of mind than a Medicare Advantage plan with a limited network.

sections.text: Avoiding Common Mistakes When Choosing a Plan

sections.content: Choosing a plan based on premium alone is one of the most common mistakes people make. A cheaper plan can carry higher copays or deductibles that cost more over a full year, especially if your health needs increase.

sections.calloutTitle: Timing Matters More Than You Think
sections.calloutText: Your Medigap Open Enrollment Period begins the month you turn 65 and are enrolled in Part B, and it lasts six months. Enrolling during this window means you cannot be denied coverage or charged more due to health conditions. Miss it, and insurers can require medical underwriting.

sections.content: Missing this enrollment window does not mean you lose access to Medigap entirely, but it can mean facing higher premiums or even denial of coverage based on your health history in many states. Marking this deadline early avoids a costly mistake later.

sections.text: How to Choose With Confidence Instead of Guessing

sections.content: A simple comparison process works better than trying to memorize every detail of every plan. Start with your current doctors, then your prescriptions, then your monthly budget, and finally your expected care needs for the coming year.

items: List your current doctors and confirm they accept Medicare
Review your prescriptions and compare Part D plan options separately
Set a realistic monthly budget for premiums
Estimate how often you expect to need care this year
Compare Plan G, N, F, A, and High Deductible G against those needs

sections.content: A licensed Medicare agent can walk through these factors with you at no cost, answering plan-specific and state-specific questions without pushing you toward a decision. Transparent guidance paired with real client experiences can make the entire process feel far less intimidating.

sections.text: Frequently Asked Questions About Medicare Supplement Plans

faqs.question: I want more time to think this through before talking to anyone. Is that okay?
faqs.answer: Absolutely. Taking time to review your options before speaking with anyone is a normal and reasonable approach. Help is available whenever you feel ready, and there is no obligation to decide quickly.

faqs.question: Will I get a pushy sales call if I ask about these plans?
faqs.answer: Licensed agents are meant to explain your options clearly, not pressure you into a decision. Transparent guidance focuses on understanding your doctors, prescriptions, and budget, not delivering a sales pitch.

faqs.question: Can I just research this on my own instead of getting help?
faqs.answer: Self-research is a great first step, and this guide is designed to support exactly that. A licensed agent can fill in the gaps once your questions become plan-specific or vary by state.

faqs.question: How do I know which source of Medicare information I can trust?
faqs.answer: Look for plain language explanations, licensed agents, and clear disclosures about how coverage works. Real client reviews and a straightforward comparison process are also strong signs that a source has your interests in mind.

sections.text: Conclusion: Choosing a Plan That Fits Your Life

sections.content: Plan G, Plan N, Plan F, Plan A, and High Deductible Plan G each serve a different combination of budget and coverage needs. None of them is universally better than the others; the right choice depends on your doctors, your prescriptions, and how much predictability you want in your monthly costs.

Comparing these factors against each plan's structure, rather than focusing on the letter alone, leads to a decision you can feel good about. When you are ready, a licensed Medicare agent can offer personalized guidance based on your specific situation and the plans available where you live.
